THIRD PARTY CROSS-DEFENDANT MAXXON CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION OF ORDER GRANTING CROSS-COMPLAINANT HOEM'S EX PARTE APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A FIRST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT ADDING MAXXON AS A CROSS-DEFENDANT IS DENIED. WHILE IT APPEARS THAT THE EX PARTE APPLICATION WAS PROCEDURALLY INCORRECT AND SHOULD HAVE BEEN FILED AS A NOTICED MOTION, MAXXON HAS NOT BEEN PREJUDICED SINCE THE MOTION, HAD IT BEEN FILED AS A NOTICED MOTION, SHOULD HAVE BEEN GRANTED COMPORTING WITH CALIFORNIA'S POLICY OF LIBERALLY ALLOWING AMENDMENTS TO PLEADINGS. THERE WAS NO UNREASONABLE DELAY BY HOEM. MAXXON WILL SUFFER NO PREJUDICE SINCE THE TRIAL DATE HAS BEEN CONTINUED TO OCTOBER 22, 2017 AND IT WILL BE ABLE TO ENGAGE IN DISCOVERY. ORDER SIGNED. ARMSTRONG ARGUES THAT IT "OWES NO DUTY" TO OTHER COMPONENT MANUFACTURERS. HOWEVER, THE CROSS-COMPLAINT IS FOR EQUITABLE INDEMNITY AND, SINCE AMERICAN MOTORCYCLE ASS'N V. SUP. CT. (1977) 20 CAL.3D 578, THAT IS OFTEN THE CASE WHEN THIS CAUSE OF ACTION IS PLED. ARMSTRONG ALSO CLAIMS UNCERTAINTY ABOUT WHAT IT IS SUED FOR, BUT THE CROSS-COMPLAINT IS ADEQUATE, PARTICULARLY GIVEN ITS INCORPORATION BY REFERENCE OF OTHER CROSS-COMPLAINTS IN THE LITIGATION. LAW AND MOTION 302, CROSS DEFENDANT MAXXON CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT. THIS MATTER IS NOT ARGUED. THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: THIRD PARTY CROSS-DEFENDANT MAXXON CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO CROSS-COMPLAINANT HOEM'S FIRST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT IS SUSTAINED WITH 10 DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND AS TO ALL CAUSES OF ACTION. THE FIRST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT FAILS TO ALLEGE SUFFICIENT FACTS SUPPORTING A CAUSE OF ACTION FOR EQUITABLE INDEMNITY, APPORTIONMENT AND CONTRIBUTION AND DECLARATORY RELIEF. HOEM IS GIVEN LEAVE TO PLEAD FACTS WITH SUFFICIENT SPECIFICITY AS TO THE PREDICATE TORT ON WHICH THE ASSERTED CAUSES OF ACTION AGAINST MAXXON ARE BASED. LACHES IS NOT A PROPER BASIS TO SUSTAIN THE DEMURER SINCE THE PLEADED FACTS IN THE FACC DO NOT SHOW PREJUDICE. (SEE BARNDT V. COUNTY OF LOS ANGELES (1989) 211 CAL.APP.3D 397, 403). ORDER SIGNED. MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION To Geo Cell Second Amended Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Feb-19-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Annalisa S. Zulueta, Esq. of G&P SCHICK PC, T:408.995.5050, for cross complainant;
No other appearance.

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on CROSS DEFENDANT MAXXON CORPORATION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION To Geo Cell's Second Amended Cross-Complaint as follows:

Defendant Maxxon Corporation's motion for summary adjudication on the second cause of action for breach of warranty in Geo Cell Solutions, Inc.'s second amended cross-complaint is denied.

The court rejects Maxxon's statute of limitations argument. The 4-year statute of limitations of Code of Civil Procedure, 337, which can implicate the discovery rule, applies to the cross-complaint. (Carrier Corp. v. Detrex Corp. (1992) 4 Cal.App.4th 1522, 1529 ["We therefore hold that in a case involving indemnification for damages under express or implied warranty based on a sales contract, the traditional time limitations for commencement of actions will prevail over California Uniform Commercial Code section 2725."].) Maxxon fails to maintain its burden of production because it presents no admissible evidence regarding when Geo Cell discovered the facts giving rise to liability.

The court rejects Maxxon's argument that it is entitled to summary adjudication based upon Geo Cell's factually devoid discovery responses. (See Andrews v. Foster Wheeler (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 103-107 [granting summary judgment in favor of asbestos defendant based upon the plaintiff's factually devoid deposition testimony and special interrogatory responses].) In this case, Geo Cell's discovery responses, including its supplementary responses, set forth facts to support the breach of warranty claim. Geo Cell contends that Maxxon's employee Josh Jonsson was present at the project, inspected the conditions, and gave his approval to install the Level-Right. Geo Cell further states that Maxxon misrepresented that the Level-Right was cementitious when it really contained high levels of gypsum. (UMF 23-24 [Geo Cell's Discovery Responses]; Taglione Depo., 419:16-420:3.) These responses are unlike the "boilerplate answers that restate their allegations, or simply provide laundry lists of people and/or documents" that the Andrews court concluded were factually devoid. (Id. at 107.) In addition, Maxxon's argument that it specifically disclaimed any implied warranties is improperly presented for the first time in its reply brief. Therefore, the motion is denied.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Severing The Cross-Complaints Of Maxxon And Armstrong And Ordering A Separate Trial
Off Calendar Jan-15-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance, or objection, the tentative ruling on CROSS COMPLAINANT ARMSTRONG FLOORING, INC.'s Motion For An Order Severing The Cross-Complaints Of Maxxon And Armstrong And Ordering A Separate Trial is adopted as follows:

Defendant Armstrong Flooring, Inc.'s motion to sever trial is denied without prejudice. Armstrong may renew the motion before the trial judge. Armstrong presently fails to demonstrate that severance of Maxxon Corporation's cross-complaint against Armstrong for indemnity will further judicial economy within the meaning of Code of Civil Procedure, 598 and 1048(b). Severance might lead to two trials and duplicative witnesses. Armstrong's motion is based on contested issues of fact and law that cannot properly be decided on this motion, and that it did not raise by moving for summary judgment. Further, if Armstrong is correct that it does not face any realistic prospect of liability at trial, it may move for a nonsuit.

Counsel for Maxxon is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Not reported.

Cross-Defendant Geo Cell Solutions, Inc.'S Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
Off Calendar Jan-10-2020 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Robert M. Gagliasso, Esq. of BUSTAMANTE & GAGLIASSO, T:408.977.1911, for cross-defendant/moving party Geo Cell Solutions;
Devin C. Courteau, Esq. of GORDON & REES, T:415.986.5900, for cross-complainant Plant Construction Co.;
Annalisa S. Zulueta, Esq. of G&P SCHICK PC, T:408.995.5050, for opposing party Maxxon;
Laura R. Grealish, Esq. of MURPHY PEARSON BRADLEY & FEENEY, T:415.788.1900;
Jimmy Ly, Esq. of WILSON ELSER MOSKOWITZ EDELMAN & DICKER LLP, T:415.433.0990, for plaintiff, on Court Call.

RULING: A R G U E D. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on Cross-Defendant Geo Cell Solutions, Inc.'S Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement, as follows:

Geo Cell Solutions, Inc.'s motion for determination of good faith settlement is granted. Based on the parties' submissions, the $5-million settlement is in good faith and within the Tech-Bilt "reasonable range." (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488; North County Contractor's Assn. v. Touchstone Ins. Services (1994) 27 Cal.App.4th 1085, 1087-91.) Seven parties are purportedly liable and the case was reportedly valued at $25 million last year. Inflated claims by partisan experts do not govern a good faith settlement motion.

Nova Partners, Inc.'s notion that Geo Cell cannot transfer indemnity rights is not held by the cases Nova cites.

As to Maxxon Corporation's arguments: (1) Geo Cell and the general contractor are allegedly joint tortfeasors, (2) a court decides setoff amounts - parties' allocations are advisory, (3) valuating indemnity rights Geo Cell is transferring would only increase the $5-million settlement, (4) Geo Cell's maintenance of "rights to its own experts" states the obvious and (5) the "$250,000 rebate" is clear and no authority barring it is cited.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Cross-Complaint
Off Calendar Dec-06-2019 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

RULING: There being no appearance, or objection, and counsel for Geo Cell having complied with the Court's conditions, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on CROSS COMPLAINANT GEO CELL SOLUTIONS, INC.'s Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Cross-Complaint, as follows:

Cross-complainant Geo-Cell Solutions, Inc.'s motion for leave to file a second amended cross-complaint is granted on the following specified conditions which Geo-Cell must agree to either orally at the hearing or in writing sent to counsel for cross-defendant Maxxon Corporation prior to the hearing. The conditions that Geo-Cell must agree to are: 1) Geo-Cell must serve verified code-compliant responses without objection other than privilege no later than December 16, 2019 to the following deem-served discovery: a) state all facts in support of all new allegations; b) state the name and contact information for each person who knows any of those facts and what facts each person knows; c) identify all documents which refer to or memorialize such facts and produce them; 2) Geo-Cell must make all persons identified in its responses who are employed by it available for a deposition of no more than three hours in length no later than January 10, 2020; and 3) Maxxon may file a summary adjudication motion directed to the new cross-claims against it to be heard no later than February 21, 2020 on 30 days' notice. If Geo-Cell does not agree to all of these conditions, the motion is denied. Granting the motion comports with California's liberal policy of allowing amendments to pleadings. The conditions on the granting of the motion eliminate any legal prejudice to Maxxon by the granting of the motion.

The Court signed the proposed order submitted by email.

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Maxxon Corporations And Armstrong Flooring, Incs Cross Complaints
Off Calendar Feb-27-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Jonathan Blute, Esq. 415-788-1900 of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney for Plaintiff.
Annalisa Zulueta, Esq. 408-995-5050 of G & P Schick for Cross-Defendant Maxxon Corporations and Armstrong Flooring Inc.
Robert Gagliasso, Esq. 408-977-1911 of Bustamante & Gagliasso APC for Cross-Defendant Geo Cell Solutions through Court Call.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views. Moving party argues procedural basis. Cross-Defendant argues. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

CROSS DEFENDANT NOVA PARTNERS, INC.'s MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Of Cross Claims of Maxxon Corporation and Armstrong flooring, Inc.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.Cross-defendant Nova Partners, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. The motion is denied as moot as to the cross-complaint by Armstrong Flooring, Inc. because that cross-complaint was dismissed on January 10, 2019. Nova's motion is denied as to the Maxxon Corporation cross-complaint because Nova failed to maintain its burden of production. The Court sustains Maxxon's objection to the Marks declaration because he failed to sign it. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 2015.5; In re Marriage of Reese & Guy (1999) 73 Cal.App.4th 1214, 1222-1223 [attorney's "custom of signing declarations under penalty of perjury on behalf of her clients and witnesses . . . [was] completely improper" and would warrant a future award of sanctions.].) Because that declaration is excluded, the consulting agreement between Nova and the University of the Pacific ("UOP")-which Nova failed timely to file with its moving papers-is not properly before the Court. In addition, Nova's motion fails on the merits. Nova fails to establish that there is no basis for liability. Page 9 of the Nova/UOP consulting agreement and Mr. Jordan's testimony show that Nova had a more active role on the project than it contends and could be comparatively at fault for the flooring problems. (Jordan Depo., 42:15-43:12; 59:14-17; 61:23-63:7.)(as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-18-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Atty. Steven Ferla of the Bustamante & Gagliasso, Tel. No. 1-408-977-1911
appearing for the Cross-Defendant by telephone appearance;
No other appearances on the matter.

RULING - The Court adopted its tentative ruling. CROSS DEFENDANT ARMSTRONG FLOORING, INC., A PENNSYLVANIA's DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT - Cross-defendant Armstrong Flooring, Inc.'s demurrer is OVERRULED. Armstrong argues that it "owes no duty" to other component manufacturers. However, the cross-complaint is for equitable indemnity and, since American Motorcycle Ass'n v. Sup. Ct. (1977) 20 Cal.3d 578, that is often the case when this cause of action is pled. Armstrong also claims uncertainty about what it is sued for, but the cross-complaint is adequate, particularly given its incorporation by reference of other cross-complaints in the litigation. Order signed in open court. Judge: Richard Ulmer; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR #8600 =302/ru

RULING: There being no objection, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on CROSS DEFENDANT MAXXON CORPORATION's Motion To Strike Second Amended Cross-Complaint Filed By Hoem And Associates, Inc., as follows:

Cross-defendant's Maxxon Corporations' motion to strike the attorney's fees allegations in the second amended cross-complaint filed by cross-complainant Hoem and Associates, Inc. is DENIED. Liberally construing the cross-complaint, Hoem has adequately alleged entitlement to fees per CCP 1021.6. At this early stage in the case, it cannot be concluded as a matter of law that Hoem's request for 1021.6 fees lacks merit.

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-11-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: CROSS DEFENDANT MAXXON CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED CROSS COMPLAINT

Appearances: Gary A. Berticevich of Ryan & Lifter (925.884.2080) appearing via Court Call for cross-complainant Hoem and Associates, Inc.; No other appearances.

This matter is not argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Third Party Cross-Defendant Maxxon Corporation's demurrer to cross-complainant Hoem's first Amended cross-complaint is sustained with 10 days leave to amend as to all causes of action. The First Amended Cross Complaint fails to allege sufficient facts supporting a cause of action for equitable indemnity, apportionment and contribution and declaratory relief. Hoem is given leave to plead facts with sufficient specificity as to the predicate tort on which the asserted causes of action against Maxxon are based. Laches is not a proper basis to sustain the demurer since the pleaded facts in the FACC do not show prejudice. (see Barndt v. County of Los Angeles (1989) 211 Cal.App.3d 397, 403). Order signed. Judge: Charlene P. Kiesselbach, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Reyes Hunter, CSR 6576 (email: rhunter@sftc.org) (302/CPK)

Off Calendar Jan-11-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: CROSS DEFENDANT MAXXON CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION OF ORDER GRANTING EX PARTE APPLICATION FOR LEAVE TO FILE A FIRST AMENDED CROSS-COMPLAINT ADDING MAXXON AS A CROSS-DEFENDANT.

Appearances: Gary A. Berticevich of Ryan & Lifter (925.884.2080) appearing via Court Call for cross-complainant Hoem and Associates, Inc.; No other appearances.

This matter is not argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Third Party Cross-Defendant Maxxon Corporation's motion for reconsideration of order granting cross-complainant Hoem's ex parte application for leave to file a first amended cross-complaint adding Maxxon as a cross-defendant is DENIED. While it appears that the ex parte application was procedurally incorrect and should have been filed as a noticed motion, Maxxon has not been prejudiced since the motion, had it been filed as a noticed motion, should have been granted comporting with California's policy of liberally allowing amendments to pleadings. There was no unreasonable delay by Hoem. Maxxon will suffer no prejudice since the trial date has been continued to October 22, 2017 and it will be able to engage in discovery. Order signed. Judge: Charlene P. Kiesselbach, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Reyes Hunter, CSR 6576 (email: rhunter@sftc.org) (302/CPK)
